title: Jaws
description: An aggressive Elwin design where the biggest points come in chunks from shark encounters and bounty hunts. Punishes sloppy play, rewards patience. That captive ball is your best friend.

summary.text: Jaws scores in chunks, not drips. Your biggest points come from completing Shark Encounter modes and Bounty Hunts. The {{callout:chum-bucket}} (center-left captive ball, between the two ramps) is your best friend — it advances the chum line toward Jaws Multiball AND lights encounter modes. Two things for one shot. Track your chum line progress by watching the row of red lit inserts that run along the right edge of the chum bucket area — each captive ball hit lights one more segment, and a full line means multiball is ready. The {{callout:fishing-reel}} (right side disc spinner) is the hardest shot on the game but you need it for bounty hunts. If you walk up cold: trap the ball, hit the captive ball, start a shark encounter, and try to complete it. That alone puts you ahead of most players at league.

one_shot.name: Chum Bucket Captive Ball
one_shot.description: Center-left of the playfield, between the wave ramp and the center ramp. This one shot advances your chum line toward Jaws Multiball AND lights encounter modes after enough hits. It's a two-for-one every time. Make it your first shot after every trap on the left flipper. If the angle feels dicey, the center ramp is your bailout — it feeds right back to the left flipper so you can try again. But the captive ball is where the game progression lives. Get comfortable with it and everything else opens up.

key_areas.name: The Chum Bucket Captive Ball
key_areas.location_hint: Center-left of the playfield, between the wave ramp on the left and the center ramp on the right.
key_areas.why_it_matters: This is the engine of the entire game. Every captive ball hit advances the chum line AND moves you toward lighting a shark encounter. You will return here after every trap on the left flipper for the whole game. If you're comfortable here, everything else flows.

key_areas.name: The Chum Line Inserts
key_areas.location_hint: The row of red inserts running along the right edge of the chum bucket area.
key_areas.why_it_matters: This is your progress bar for Jaws Multiball. Each captive ball hit lights one more segment. A full row means multiball is ready to start — you'll also see it on the display, but the physical inserts are the fastest read mid-ball. Checking this takes one glance and tells you exactly where you are in the game.

key_areas.name: The Fin Drop Target
key_areas.location_hint: Center playfield, motorized — it moves along the chum line area.
key_areas.why_it_matters: The fin is Step 2 of starting Jaws Multiball and Step 2 of every bounty hunt. Hit yellow shots to load the harpoon, then shoot the fin to attach a barrel. It's also the single most dangerous drain spot on the table — balls ricochet off it unpredictably toward the center. Knowing where it is and respecting the ricochet is essential.

key_areas.name: The Fishing Reel Spinner
key_areas.location_hint: Right side of the playfield, just left of the right ramp entrance.
key_areas.why_it_matters: The hardest shot on the game and required for bounty hunts. It's a disc spinner, not a traditional wire spinner, which makes the timing different. You don't need to master it on Ball 1, but knowing where it sits and that it requires a specific timing approach is worth having in your head before you need it.

key_areas.name: The Mini-Flipper
key_areas.location_hint: Lower right of the playfield, below the bumper.
key_areas.why_it_matters: Trap balls here to access left-side shots — including the harpoon lane for quickshots. During multiball, hold the mini-flipper and press the action button to flip-lock a ball for ~15 seconds, turning chaotic multiball into controlled single-ball play. Learning to use this intentionally separates comfortable Jaws players from frustrated ones.

hard_box.title: What Makes This Game Hard
hard_box.text: Jaws plays aggressively. Balls ricochet off the {{callout:fin-target}} unpredictably — this is the single most common drain spot on the game. The right side of the playfield funnels toward the right outlane more than you'd like. Modes have strict time limits that punish hesitation. The good news? The {{callout:mini-flipper}} gives you access to left-side shots including the harpoon lane, and the life ring (action button) saves you from left outlane drains once you learn the timing. Those two skills — using the mini-flipper and timing the life ring — are worth more than any combo strategy on this machine.

priorities.title: Hit the Skill Shot
priorities.description: Plunge toward the top bumper for a free 5M (Level 1 skill shot). Don't overthink Level 2 — it's inconsistent. If you've already gotten Level 1, try the secret skill shot: plunge softly so the ball rolls down to the flippers, then hit the Chum Bucket Captive Ball without touching anything else first for a bonus award.

priorities.title: Shoot the Chum Bucket Every Trap
priorities.description: Every time you trap on the left flipper, your first move is the Chum Bucket Captive Ball. It lights red chum shots (advancing the chum line inserts toward Jaws Multiball) AND progresses toward encounter mode availability. Watch those red inserts fill up — that's your game state at a glance. If the captive ball angle feels dicey, shoot the Center Ramp (the U-turn ramp to the right of the chum bucket, feeds back to the left flipper) to reset your position and try again.

priorities.title: Start a Shark Encounter — Pick Scars First
priorities.description: After hitting the captive ball enough times, white shots light up around the playfield — each one starts a different encounter mode. Pick Scars first if it's available: it uses the Center Ramp, it's designed for single-ball play, and completing it permanently increases your machete multiplier. Raft Attack (right ramp) is your second choice. Save Night Swim and Beach Panic for later — they're designed to be stacked with multiball.

priorities.title: Collect Red Chum Shots When They're In Your Path
priorities.description: After captive ball hits, red arrows light up on various shots around the playfield. Collect them when they're in the direction you're already shooting — they fill chum line segments and accelerate your progress toward Jaws Multiball. Don't go hunting them across the table. Progress carries over between balls, so every segment you light tonight stays lit.

priorities.title: Start Jaws Multiball If the Chum Line Is Full
priorities.description: Check the Chum Line Inserts when you plunge — if all segments are lit, you're ready. Shoot any yellow-lit shot to load the harpoon (you'll hear a click and see the harpoon indicator on the display light up), then quickly shoot the Fin Drop Target (the moving motorized fin, center playfield) to attach the barrel. Then hit the flashing yellow shot to start multiball. Don't panic-flip after hitting the fin — it causes unpredictable ricochets. Stay calm and track the ball.

priorities.title: Stack a Mode With Jaws Multiball 1
priorities.description: Jaws Multiball 1 doesn't score much on its own. Use it as a safety net to complete a shark encounter instead — start the encounter before or after starting multiball, either order works. Beach Panic (wave ramp) and Night Swim (left orbit) are ideal stack targets because they have many lit shots spread across the playfield, so random multiball hits still make progress.

priorities.title: Try Quickshots From the Mini-Flipper
priorities.description: When the ball lands on the Mini-Flipper (lower right, below the bumper), shoot the harpoon lane (the narrow lane in the upper-left, accessible from the mini-flipper backhand). This sets up a quickshot at the fin target. Three quickshots build toward an extra ball, and each one lights gear for collection. The multiplier resets when you miss one — don't force it if the shot isn't there today.

priorities.title: Start Your First Bounty Hunt — Pick Mako
priorities.description: Two right ramp shots (or enough Fishing Reel Spinner spins) light a bounty hunt. Always pick Mako first — completing it upgrades the reel, making future reel shots easier to time. The bounty hunt starts with harpoon loading (yellow shots) and barrel attachment (the Fin Drop Target), same sequence as starting Jaws Multiball. The reel timing tip from Kineticist: trap the ball on the left flipper, flip right when the ball overlaps the left edge of the Jaws logo insert between the flippers.

priorities.title: Check Chum Line and Multiball Status Immediately
priorities.description: Glance at the Chum Line Inserts right after plunging. If you're one or two segments from full, go straight to the captive ball before doing anything else. If Jaws Multiball is already ready (all inserts lit), go for the harpoon/barrel sequence immediately. Don't waste Ball 3 rebuilding from scratch when you might already be close.

priorities.title: Complete Any Encounters You Have Running
priorities.description: Any encounter modes that were lit or in progress carry over to Ball 3. Finish them before starting new ones. A completed encounter pays more than an abandoned one and can chain into follow-up scoring. On Ball 3, completion beats starting-over.

priorities.title: Go for a Bounty Hunt If Mako Is Already Captured
priorities.description: If Mako is done and the Fishing Reel Spinner is upgraded, Ball 3 is the time to attempt Thresher or Hammerhead. The reel is your barrier — if it's feeling right tonight, push for the bounty. If it's been inconsistent, stay on encounters and chum. No shame in playing your strengths on Ball 3.

items.title: Time the Life Ring — Don't Panic Press
items.description: The action button deploys a life ring ball save from the left outlane. The timing matters — press it when the ball is actually entering the Left Outlane (the gap to the left of the left flipper), not when it's still bouncing around elsewhere. Too early and it saves nothing. Kineticist calls this crucial to master and they're right. It's a free save once per ball; learn the exact moment to use it.

items.title: Center Ramp Is Your Bailout
items.description: When you're unsure, panicking, or the ball is moving fast — shoot the Center Ramp (U-turn ramp right of the chum bucket, feeds back to the left flipper). It resets your position to a trap with options. It almost always does something useful in the background too. This is your safe shot every single time.

items.title: Fin Ricochets Are Coming — Be Ready
items.description: Every time you shoot the Fin Drop Target, brace both flippers immediately after. The ball bounces off the fin unpredictably toward center drain. This is the most common 'what just happened' drain on the game. It's not bad luck — it's a known hazard. Anticipate it every time.

items.title: Flip-Lock During Multiball
items.description: During any multiball, trap a ball on the Mini-Flipper and press the action button (it flashes green when flip-lock is available) to hold it there for ~15 seconds. This turns chaotic 3-ball multiball into manageable single-ball play. Use the window to aim at jackpots or mode shots instead of flailing at everything.

items.title: Pond Attack on a Bad Reel Night
items.description: Pond Attack requires consistent Fishing Reel Spinner shots — the hardest shot on the game. If the reel isn't going in tonight, skip this encounter entirely. Starting it and missing the reel repeatedly burns the timer, stresses you out, and usually ends in a drain. Work around it.

items.title: Night Swim in Single-Ball Play
items.description: Night Swim has too many required shots with a strict timer for comfortable single-ball execution. It's designed to be stacked with a multiball where extra balls give you a safety net. If it's Ball 1 or Ball 2 with no multiball in sight, don't start it.

items.title: Chasing the Fishing Reel on Ball 1
items.description: Don't spend Ball 1 practicing the Fishing Reel Spinner. It's the skill this game rewards most at advanced levels, but on Ball 1 it just drains you while you calibrate. Get your chum line started and run an encounter. Come back to the reel on Ball 2 once you have a feel for the machine's speed.

items.title: Forcing the Barrel Sequence When Not Ready
items.description: The harpoon-load → Fin Drop Target barrel sequence to start Jaws Multiball has a tight timer between steps. If you're flustered, if the fin feels out of position, or if the ball is rolling awkwardly — let the timer expire and reset. A failed barrel attach causes a dangerous fin ricochet at the worst possible moment. It resets cleanly; take the do-over.

between_us.label: The Chum Line Is Your Entire Ball 1 Goal
between_us.text: Every captive ball hit does two things simultaneously: fills a segment of the chum line (watch those red inserts) and advances encounter mode availability. There's no situation in Ball 1 where not hitting the captive ball is the right call. Even a partial chum line at the end of Ball 1 is progress that carries over.

between_us.label: Jaws Multiball 1 Scores Less Than You Think
between_us.text: Players often sacrifice good single-ball play to rush into Jaws Multiball 1 because it feels like the big scoring event. It isn't — the jackpots are modest. MB1 is most valuable as a safety net for completing an encounter mode, not as a standalone scoring event. MB2 (The Chase) is the real prize. Build toward that.

between_us.label: Gear Items Actually Matter
between_us.text: After completing encounters and quickshots, gear items become available for collection at certain shots. Shark Cage gives a one-time fin ricochet save (useful). Dart makes bounty progression easier. Don't ignore these — they're the game rewarding you for doing the right things, and some of them meaningfully change what you can attempt on the next ball.

between_us.label: Pro vs. Premium/LE Differences
between_us.text: On the Pro: no upper playfield, standup targets instead of drop targets in some locations, captive ball with Bruce the shark instead of the bash toy boat. The core strategy is identical across models — captive ball, encounters, chum line, bounty hunts. The Premium/LE upper playfield adds shots but doesn't change what you should be doing on Ball 1.

playfield_callouts.name: Chum Bucket Captive Ball
playfield_callouts.description: Center-left of the playfield, between the wave ramp (left) and center ramp (right). Your first shot every time you trap on the left flipper. Advances chum line toward Jaws Multiball and lights shark encounter modes. Two-for-one, every time.

playfield_callouts.name: Chum Line Inserts
playfield_callouts.description: The row of red lit inserts running along the right edge of the chum bucket area. Each captive ball hit lights one more segment. A fully lit row means Jaws Multiball is ready to start. This is your fastest read of game state — one glance tells you how far along you are without reading the display.

playfield_callouts.name: Fishing Reel Spinner
playfield_callouts.description: Right side of the playfield, just left of the right ramp entrance. The hardest shot on the game — a disc spinner, not a traditional wire spinner. Timing tip from Kineticist: trap on left flipper, flip right when the ball overlaps the left edge of the Jaws logo insert between the flippers. Required for bounty hunts and Pond Attack encounter.

playfield_callouts.name: Fin Drop Target
playfield_callouts.description: The motorized shark fin that moves along the chum line area, center playfield. Yellow shots load the harpoon; then shoot this target to attach a barrel toward Jaws Multiball or a bounty hunt. WARNING: balls ricochet unpredictably off this target toward center drain. Brace both flippers immediately after every fin hit.

playfield_callouts.name: Mini-Flipper
playfield_callouts.description: Lower right of the playfield, below the bumper and buoy. Trap balls here to access left-side shots including the harpoon lane. During multiball, hold the mini-flipper and press the green flashing action button to flip-lock a ball for ~15 seconds. Some copies of the machine leak balls from here toward the right outlane — know your machine.

playfield_callouts.name: Center Ramp
playfield_callouts.description: The U-turn ramp between the chum bucket and the wave ramp, center of playfield. Feeds back to the left flipper. Your bailout shot when unsure or under pressure. Also starts the Scars encounter (best first mode for single-ball play) and contributes to chum line progress via red chum shot collection.

playfield_callouts.name: Left Outlane
playfield_callouts.description: The gap to the left of the left flipper. Press the action button when the ball enters here to deploy the life ring ball save. Timing is critical — press when the ball is actually in the outlane, not before. One save available per ball.

playfield_callouts.name: Left Standup Targets
playfield_callouts.description: Three standup targets on the left side of the playfield. Completing them lights your machete multiplier (2x at the inlanes) and advances fish finder progress. Hittable from the mini-flipper backhand or a right-flipper shot toward the left side.

sources.title: The Pinball Company — Travis's Jaws Tutorial
sources.description: Primary video source. Travis's complete walkthrough including skill shot technique, encounter mode selection, bailout shot philosophy, and multiball management.

sources.title: Kineticist — Jaws Strategy Guide
sources.description: Primary written source. Noah Crable's detailed breakdown including fishing reel timing technique, life ring critical timing, and gear item strategy.

sources.title: Tilt Forums — Jaws Rulesheet
sources.description: Community rulesheet with detailed scoring data and mode timer information.

sources.title: Pinside — Jaws Owners Club
sources.description: Community discussion on bounty hunt sequencing, gear item value, and multiball stacking strategies.

chat_starters: How do I know when Jaws Multiball is ready to start?
Which shark encounter should I pick first?
What's the fishing reel timing trick?
How does the flip-lock work during multiball?

level_up.intro: Once the chum line rhythm feels automatic and you're completing encounters consistently, these strategies will push your scores significantly higher.

tips.title: Stack Beach Panic or Night Swim With Jaws Multiball 1
tips.description: Jaws MB1 scores poorly on its own — treat it as a utility multiball. Start an encounter first (or have one ready to start), then launch multiball. Beach Panic is ideal because it has shots lit across the entire playfield so random multiball hits still make progress. Use flip-lock to manage ball count while aiming at mode shots.

tips.title: Feeding Frenzy During Multiball
tips.description: During Jaws Multiball, spray yellow standup targets to trigger Feeding Frenzy in the background. The left spinner is key — every spin counts as a switch hit building the Feeding Frenzy value. The third target in each set pays 3x the built-up value. This adds to end-of-ball bonus permanently, so it compounds across balls.

tips.title: Machete Timing
tips.description: Machetes are 2x+ multipliers applied to your next shot after rolling through a lit inlane. Best moments to cash them: on the final Scars encounter shot, during an encounter jackpot, on the last target of a lit Feeding Frenzy set, or on a beach closed hurry-up. Don't waste them on casual captive ball hits or random chum shots.

tips.title: Jaws Multiball 2 (The Chase) Is the Real Prize
tips.description: MB2 is far more valuable than MB1. Reach it by attaching a second barrel — complete the left standup targets when 'Ready Barrel' is lit, then repeat the harpoon/fin sequence. During The Chase, switch hits light all major shots for jackpots — the left spinner is the fastest way to light them. This is the scoring event the whole game builds toward.

tips.title: Bounty Hunt Sequencing
tips.description: Always capture Mako first — it upgrades the fishing reel, making future reel timing easier. Collect the Dart gear item before starting your second bounty hunt; it increases the bounty value and makes progression easier. Thresher or Hammerhead next depending on your shot confidence. Tiger Shark is the hardest but awards unlimited flip-lock for the rest of the game — massive if you reach it.
